experience: Participating in the communal hand-pulled noodle breakfast at a Lanzhou Muslim noodle shop where the lamian pulling happens at the counter and first-time customers are expected to specify their noodle thickness from a chart of 9 widths
where: Old city Lanzhou, Gansu Province — specifically the century-old shops near Zhangye Road
when: 6am–9am; the breakfast window is strict and shops often sell out
why_distinctive: The 9-thickness specification ritual (from capellini-fine to belt-wide) is a genuine ordering convention with social weight — locals judge newcomers on their choice. The pulling happens in open view as a performance. This is not a tourist demonstration but a functional production process.
source: Referenced in Carolyn Phillips' 'All Under Heaven' (2016, Ten Speed Press) in the context of northwestern Muslim-Chinese food traditions; also documented in a 2019 Roads & Kingdoms piece on Gansu food by multiple contributors. NOTE: verify Roads & Kingdoms piece independently.

pass2_verification.concerns: Carolyn Phillips and 'All Under Heaven' (2016, Ten Speed Press) are real and recognized — the book is a comprehensive survey of Chinese regional cuisines including northwestern Muslim-Chinese traditions, making it a plausible source for Lanzhou lamian context. However, it is a broad cookbook and may mention Lanzhou noodles only briefly rather than documenting the specific experiential details claimed (9-width chart, 6–9am sell-out window, counter-pulling for first-timers). The Roads & Kingdoms piece is flagged by the claim itself as unverified; Roads & Kingdoms is a real publication that has covered Chinese regional food, and a 2019 Gansu piece is plausible but not independently confirmable here. The 9-width thickness chart and strict breakfast window are highly specific details that are consistent with real Lanzhou beef noodle culture but would need source-level confirmation to verify attribution. The claim may be accurate as a travel experience while overstating how directly these specifics appear in the cited sources.
